The table above shows how half or 11 out of the 22 stocks listed above managed to beat XLE in terms of gains in Q1 2026, led by APA with a gain of 73.51%, TPL with a gain of 65.23% and OXY with a gain of 58.07%. Still, not all energy stocks fared well in what was an exceptional quarter because EXE lost 0.53% during the quarter.

This goes up to 3 out of 22 that lost ground if only the month of March, or when the war in the Middle East got underway, is considered, namely TPL, BKR and WMB. Another two, SLB and KMI came very close to ending March in the red. APA was the standout performer with a gain of 39.74% in March, well ahead of runner-ups MPC with a gain of 23.19% and OXY with a gain of 22.46%.

In other words, if APA is excluded as it was very much an outlier in March, XLE did not benefit as much from the outbreak of the fighting, in contrast to oil futures. If someone wanted to take optimal advantage of the present situation in energy markets and the war in the Middle East in terms of returns, then playing crude oil itself was the way to go. Energy stocks, not so much.

What to look for in Q2 2026

The outperformance on the part of the energy sector stands out because it could have turned out very differently if there had been no war in the Middle East. Both the IEA and OPEC called for the oil market to be oversupplied heading into 2026, but the restrictions imposed by Iran on ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz have turned the supply and demand equation completely upside down.

It’s clear that if not for the geopolitical factor, energy stocks would have fared worse than they did in Q1 2026. It is the war in the Middle East that is pushing up the sector and with the war still ongoing, it is tempting to place bets on energy stocks in Q2 2026. However, how Q2 2026 turns out is not pre-determined as there are a number of factors that could sway how the quarter turns out.

There have been attacks on energy-related infrastructure throughout the Middle East that could have a lingering impact. Qatar, for example, has announced that some of the damage done to its LNG facilities could take years to repair. If this continues and more production facilities go offline, energy markets will remain in a crisis even if the Strait of Hormuz is fully re-opened.

Given the current situation, crude prices are on the rise and they may very well have yet to peak. One of the more immediate measures taken after the fighting broke out was for countries to release supplies from oil reserves. This includes the U.S. with its strategic petroleum reserve. However, unless more reserves are made available, the impact is set to recede in Q2 2026.

It has also been a month since the war started, which means most of the tankers currently stuck in the Persian Gulf were supposed to arrive at their destination sometime in Q2 2026, had there been no war. While some have been able to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Q2 2026 is when the market will start feeling the impact of tankers not making delivery. All this suggests oil and gas prices are likely to rise much higher in Q2 2026.

However, while it is tempting to bet on higher energy prices, especially with the prior points in mind, higher prices are still not a foregone conclusion, let alone any specific number, because it depends on how the war in the Middle East evolves. Iran, for instance, has effectively seized control of the Strait of Hormuz, which allows it to decide how much oil enters world markets.

This means Iran can increase global prices, but Iran can also decrease prices if it increases the number of ships that have been allowed to cross. While Iran benefits from higher oil prices, Iran also needs to have certain countries on its side, China in particular. Iran has a better chance of achieving this if it allows supplies to these countries to pass through. This will constrain the rise of global energy prices, at least to a certain extent.

Still, no factor is likely to exert greater sway in terms of how Q2 2026 turns out for the energy sector than how the war itself evolves. At this time, the most probable scenario is for higher energy prices, but this can change if the war is perceived to be close to an end. The U.S. is trying to degrade Iran’s ability to launch attacks to the point that it can no project power into the region, which includes damaging facilities in the region and blocking the Strait of Hormuz.

But whether the U.S. succeeds in doing this in Q2 2026 is up in the air. According to most pre-war estimates, Iran was believed to have an arsenal of several thousands of ballistic missiles. If this is accurate, and it can be assumed that a portion is no longer available, either because they have been used by Iran or destroyed by the U.S./Israel, then it is possible Iran may run out or at least run short sometime in Q2 2026. This would affect Iran’s ability to continue the war. Iran also has drones, but these are not as difficult to stop. A defeated Iran would cause energy prices to crater and energy stocks would not be far behind.

Yet it needs mentioning that the numbers are just estimates and it is not impossible that Iran has a much greater inventory than believed. Iran can manufacture its own weapons, although it probably needs to import certain components. On the other hand, the same can be said of the U.S. since it too is expending its inventory of weapons.

If the war devolves into a war of attrition and both sides seek to outlast the other, Iran will need to find a way to get re-supplied and replenish what has been expended. This is where Russian and China can play a key role because with their support, Iran stands a much better chance of getting the supplies it needs to continue the war. Russia in particular has a lot of reasons for wanting Iran to continue to fight.

The goal for the U.S. is to degrade Iran’s military to the point that it is effectively defeated, but for Iran the goal is to deny the U.S. from achieving this objective, whether or not in collaboration with other countries. If Iran is to come out on top, it will probably require China’s and Russia’s support. They don’t have to get directly involved, but they do need to keep supplies flowing to Iran to allow it to continue the fight.

What this all suggests that while the most probable outcome is for energy stocks to move higher in Q2 2026, it is not a guaranteed bet as the possibility exists for energy prices to go down and take the energy sector down with it. While the first looks more likely given the present situation, either outcome is possible in Q2 2026.

Why there could be a further divergence between energy prices and energy stocks

There is another scenario worth taking into account when deciding whether to be betting on the energy sector in Q2 2026. It is not impossible for the energy sector to lag behind energy prices, even if the war intensifies and energy prices soar higher. Keep in mind that ETF’s like XLE consists primarily of U.S. companies.

The issue here is that Iran could specifically target U.S. interests, which would hurt American companies and that could affect how XLE trades since attacks will lead to financial losses for companies. Iran is already doing this to some extent, including by letting say oil tankers from certain countries unscathed, while not doing the same to those tied to the U.S.

In the worst case, Iran could be perceived to be winning the what is currently undecided conflict. If this happens, the long-term consequences would be very negative for not just the U.S., but also U.S. companies doing business in the region and that includes those in XLE. There is more at risk for XLE than the headline gains for XLE in Q1 2026 may indicate.

Why the oil crisis may come back to haunt the energy sector in the long term

How Q2 2026 ultimately plays out has yet to be determined, both in terms of the war itself, energy prices and the energy sector. It is not guaranteed, but energy bulls have the better odds as to who gets rewarded in Q1 2026, as things currently stand. However, with that said, there is reason to think that even if the energy sector, as covered by XLE, continues to be a winner in the short term, it might also lose out in the long term.

The current environment of very high energy prices is very unlikely to illicit no response. One consequence is that there will be renewed attempts to reduce reliance on the Middle East for energy supplies, both oil and gas. Some of the Gulf States are likely to try to reduce their reliance on the Strait of Hormuz by creating alternative transport routes using pipelines, but this cannot be a final solution because pipelines are still subject to attacks from drones or what not that can disrupt supplies.

If Iran’s leverage with the Strait of Hormuz is to be reduced, it will require alternative sources of energy that do not reside in the region. This means more investments, especially from the oil majors, in exploration and/or exploitation. Capex is likely to increase and that could impact the balance sheet. North America is likely to play a key role when it comes to increasing supplies outside of the Middle East.

The goal is to increase supplies from outside the Middle East so as the reduce potential fallout from anything happening in that specific region. There is a historical precedent for this happening. For instance, OPEC supplied slightly more than half the world’s oil in 1973 when the oil crisis erupted later that year. The resulting oil embargo drove Western countries in particular to look for alternative sources, which eventually resulted in OPEC gradually losing market share in the following decades.

OPEC’s response was to bring in other countries, Russia in particular, by creating OPEC+, or an expanded set of oil-producing countries. This restored the new entity’s ability to influence prices by controlling roughly half of world production at the time it was founded. If history repeats and the reaction to the current oil shock is similar to the oil shock of the seventies, energy supplies from non-traditional actors are likely to grow quickly in the coming years. The Guyana basin is a candidate here.

The flip side of this is that it could lead to depressed prices as the market could get oversupplied. OPEC+ and non-member suppliers could begin to duke it out. This would hurt the energy sector by essentially recreating the conditions that had become rather prevalent before the current oil shock provided relief. So while energy stocks may be celebrating what is currently going on in the world, they may not be a few down years down the road when consequences from the latest oil stock start to make their impact felt.

Another way XLE could get adversely affected worth mentioning is by increased production from renewable energy sources like solar energy. On the demand side, there will likely be attempts to drive down the need for oil and gas with the use of electrical vehicles and so on. The current energy crisis, which has led to higher prices and/or shortages, will increase the incentive to switch towards alternative energy sources that are less likely to be disrupted by a war in the Middle East. This will affect an ETF like XLE that is weighted towards companies active in the oil and gas sector with XOM and CVX accounting for 41% by weight.

Investor takeaways

The energy sector was a clear winner in Q1 2026, thanks to an assist from recent developments in the Middle East, and it is tempting to bet on a repeat of Q1 2026 in Q2 2026. The war continues and while the U.S. would probably like there to be a halt to the fighting, provided it leads to the restoration of the situation as it was before the war, which includes the free movement of ships through the Strait of Hormuz, the same may not necessarily be true of Iran.

Iran is more likely to see a continuation of the war in its interest because the full impact of the disruptions to the oil and gas market in the past 6 weeks or so have yet to be felt. The Gulf States are said to be running low on interceptors to defend themselves against Iranian attacks, which means that attacks on energy infrastructure and so on could become more effective in Q2 2026 compared to Q1 2026.

The U.S. is busy degrading Iran’s military, but there is no sign that Iran has lost the ability to fight. This favors a continuation of the war in Q2 2026, which in turn favors upwards movement in energy prices and the energy sector by extension. It is thus tempting to be long energy stocks, like those represented in an ETF like XLE, but there is a risk to doing so because of the possibility the war could go in a direction that is not readily apparent at the start of Q2 2026. This includes an end, whether temporary or not, to the fighting.

This would not be well received by the energy sector, especially since without this war, the energy sector would likely be underperforming, like it did before. While this is arguably less likely to happen than the more likely scenario of higher energy prices due to continued fighting, it is a possibility that cannot be ruled out and therefore must be accounted for.

How the war itself evolves will determine the path of energy prices, and by extension what kind of quarter it will be for the energy sector in Q2 2026. I would therefore hold on to existing positions, but I would not be a buyer of more shares, not after the way they have rallied by as much as they already have. It is not impossible for such a move to pay off, but it depends too much on something happening, which is for the war to continue, something not at all guaranteed. If for whatever reason this does not happen, the energy sector can expect to perform like it did in recent quarters, which is not that good.

data.title: TYG: Can See Durable Growth Over The Coming Decade
data.description:
The Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp (TYG) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with exposure to master limited partnerships [MLPs] in the energy infrastructure market, earning investors capital growth and distribution income. The strategy employs leverage, allowing the fund to provide enhanced exposure to the market while facilitating a higher earning potential. With increasing demand for natural gas resources in the US and overseas, I believe the next wave of growth investments will set the stage for renewed momentum in the industry, presenting investors with an appealing investment approach to capital-heavy, oil & gas midstream companies. Given the potential market tailwinds to come, I am recommending TYG with a Buy rating.

Investment Case for TYG

TYG currently provides investors with exposure to midstream gas as well as power infrastructure, a market with an appealing backdrop underpinned by growing electricity demand in the US. Driving demand growth is the combination of reindustrialization and the development of large-scale data centers to power AI. Thematically, these two factors coincide as both a user of AI and a developer/facilitator of AI. From the industrial standpoint, the market firmly believes that new and existing facilities will lean into automation and industrial robotics to deliver factory output optimization, which may inadvertently lead to increased power needs to operate these autonomous machines.

The data center growth story presents substantial demand for electricity with a single compound requiring the amount of power to light up a small city. Many of the facilities being constructed are 200MW+ with larger facilities being constructed with 1GW+ of capacity, potentially requiring a dedicated, behind-the-meter power supply before transitioning to grid load. Regardless of the path of power, more infrastructure will be necessary to light up these facilities, driving a renewed growth path for companies operating the infrastructure and producing the power.

Another growth theme that is emerging as a more critical component of the domestic gas theme is how the Iranian war may shape the future of LNG supply. Prior to the war, Qatar supplied 20% of the world’s LNG cargoes before missile attacks and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Deepening the global gas uncertainty, gas fields and infrastructure have been a primary target between both war powers with Saudi Arabia and the UAE also halting production following a missile strike on Iranian gas fields.

While we shouldn’t expect an immediate impact to the domestic gas market, I believe that if the Iranian war progresses beyond weeks/months, the supply chain for LNG cargoes will need to be rewritten to derisk regional exposure, potentially creating an appealing opportunity for domestic exporters to accelerate the development of new liquefaction facilities. This, in turn, can deliver growth across the value chain, particularly for interstate pipeline and natural gas gathering and processing operators. Though planning and development may take years before capital commitments, the shift towards growth can be appealing for long-term investors, particularly when considering the long-term distributable cash flow growth that may come.

Overall, companies held in TYG should benefit from the growing demand for electricity, driving the need for growth capital investments that can be viewed as long-term expansionary for cash generation.

About Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp

TYG was launched on February 24, 2004 by Tortoise Capital as a closed-end fund. The strategy has a management fee of 95bps, not inclusive of leverage fees and other operating fees.

The strategy is more heavily allocated to common shares at 78% with MLPs accounting for 20% of the portfolio weight; when applying leverage, TYG provided 101.5% exposure to common shares and 26% exposure to MLPs as of December 31, 2025. TYG employs a leverage rate of 24.6% to enhance asset exposure, allowing the fund to provide greater investment opportunities to its shareholders.

Breaking down the portfolio, TYG is most heavily exposed to renewables and power infrastructure, accounting for 54.2% of common equity exposure. Top holdings within the industry include Clearway Energy (CWEN), Evergy (EVRG), and Sempra Energy (SRE), providing exposure to diversified power production in renewable resources, natural gas, and coal.

As for midstream infrastructure, TYG provides significant exposure to Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX (MPLX), and The Williams Companies (WMB), providing investors with diversified exposure to natural gas pipelines and gathering & processing as well as refined products pipelines.

In addition to owning shares and units, TYG may write call options on shares held in order to enhance distributions throughout the year. In FY25, TYG wrote call options on Core Scientific (CORZ), First Solar (FSLR), and Femi.

TYG transitioned to a monthly distribution at the start of 2025 after merging with the Tortoise Midstream Energy Fund. Distributions have exhibited annual growth since 2021 following the near collapse of the US O&G market. The distribution presently pays out $5.70/share, yielding 12.25% on a forward basis, presenting an appealing investment opportunity for income-seeking investors. A major advantage to holding TYG is that income is reported on a 1099 rather than a K-1.

Risks Related to TYG

TYG is a non-diversified portfolio strategy that is heavily exposed to the energy commodity market, presenting certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. While many midstream operators have transitioned towards a fee-based revenue strategy, earning tariff fees on transported volumes, the market may be exposed to the impacts of negative commodity pricing performance, particularly if volume production were to be scaled back. Investors will be exposed to manager risk, relying on the abilities of the portfolio management team to appropriately select and weight equities and units. Distributions may exhibit a high return of capital given the nature of distributable income across the portfolio holdings.

Final Thoughts

TYG can be an appealing investment strategy for investors seeking domestic energy exposure, particularly when considering the growth outlay for supplying capacity for the increased shift in power demand tied to reindustrialization and large-scale data center development. While TYG may not offer the same level of high-growth as other sectors, the high level of distributed income and durability brings a certain appeal that can provide investors with more stability in their portfolio strategies. Given that this is a non-diversified portfolio, TYG should be held as a satellite position as part of a diversified portfolio; I am recommending TYG with a Buy rating.
